EDITORIAL.

Last week we pleaded for early action to
be taken in regatd to the propoied Wolfe
Tone Memorial Tournament, and we are
now desicous of adding a few farther words
to that ples. This week-end « quarterly
meeting of the Central Council will be held
in Dublin, and we would strongly urge
that body to make a start with the project,
and thus redeem their promise of over a
year's standing. Before the next quarterly
meeting is convened we shall have entered
upon new year, and there would not be
a slficient margin of time for the efficient
organising and carrying through of ‘the
Tournament before the 1915 Champion-
ships were due for decision. He

clear the present mecting should deal with
the matter if it is to be dealt wich at all
this year, To our mind, there has been
quite enough delay already, and we know
(of no sufficient reason for any further put-
tung off of the project. If we are in earnest
about this patriotic work, it were fuer for

Us to be doing something practical, here and
now, rather than to be postponing it to some
later date, We hope to see that the quar-
terly meeting will take some action cowards
forwarding the Tournament.

~ - -

‘Another matter which we understand
will come up for consideration at the mect

ing is the resolution regarding the forma-
tion and affiliation of Rifle Clubs under
G.A.A. auspices forwarded by Cork and
Kerry County Boards. Since we last wrote
upon this matter a rather startling trans
formation bas taken place in Irish public
affairs owing to the “ split” in the ranks of
the Trish Volunteers. We wish to say,
though, that this effee:s no alteration in
the desirability, of Rifle Clubs being formed
under G.A.A. control. It gives rise, how-
ever, to one important consideration, which
must never de lost sight of. Volunteers

~ +

-

The Volunteer “split” is nothing short
of aNational caustrophe. Here we had
iinmeasurably the finest National effort
directed towards the regaining of our poll
tieal freedom which we have witnessed for
over a century. A buge National force
was rapidly arsing amongst us which could
toon defy with impunity any foreign Power
atraying itself agains us. Now we sec it
shattered, impotent. Ic is pitiable to reflect
tupon the ruined tangle of broken wheels
and useless scrap to which our splendid
fighting machine has now been reduced. It
seems idle to talk of reconstructing the
Volunteers, no matter what party attempts
the task. Now ,that it becomes purely &
arty question, we must refrain from eriti-
though we are carefully watching
events in order to discover from whence the
despicable Imperialism emanaced which re-
cently discovered itself in the movement,
We should feel very contented if it were
ponible to. discover the true feeling of the
Fankand file on the whole issue, by obviating.
the political thimble-rigging aad resolution
faking which goes to making up » modern
partyccampaign. We wonder if such»
Consummation is possible, Both sides speak
of holding Volunteer conventions —a rather
belated procedure. Could not one really
representative gathering be held, of which
the delegates should be selected under the
impartial supervision of G.A.A. County
organizations, and all parties abide by the
result?

Overdoing Athletics.

SOME DANGERS.

Overdoing athletic sports becomes a dan-
ger in summer. There is as much danger
fn taking too long walks or too long swims
as there is in straining in a_ boat race or
football match. There is a widespread idea
that each form of athletics develops its own
ies, “bicycle heart,” “football kid-
‘&e, This is nor true
thletic exercises may be divided into
feats of strength and feats of endurance.
eve do not differ essentially in their effect
on the body. There are_no important
diflzrences between the different types of
athletic sports in respect to their physiologic
effects on the body. ‘The severe symptoms,
however, make their appearance more pro-
minently in sports like football, wrestling,
contesis, etc., which may call for

Age, a Factor.

To what degree the distinctly harmful
results of improper athletics may arise de~
pends on a variety of circumstanres. Age
ws a factor of significance, During the
period of boyhoos, when the organs bave
rot reached their full development, the per
